title: Tools and methods for monitoring the health of the urban greenery

data_scope.supplementary_tables: Supplementary Tables 1 and 2 compile all reviewed methods with resolution, cost, and invasiveness ratings
data_scope.sensor_examples: FLIR Lepton 3.5 (8-14 micrometres), MAPIR Survey 3W (660/550/850 nm), Apple iPhone 12 Pro + Polycam LiDAR

core_conclusion: No single monitoring technology resolves the fundamental space-time resolution trade-off in urban greenery health assessment: embedded sensors provide high temporal granularity per tree but at high cost for city-wide coverage, while satellite and airborne imaging covers large areas at lower temporal frequency. Combining multiple sensing modalities — physical sensors, thermal/hyperspectral imaging, LiDAR, and citizen science — can produce hyper-local greenery health data that directly supports six Sustainable Development Goal use cases, from selective watering and early disease detection to urban heat island mitigation.
why_it_matters: Bridges the $2 trillion urban tree maintenance gap with a systematic method-selection framework; links tree health monitoring directly to SDG delivery across SDGs 3, 4, 8, 10, 11, 13, 15, and 17; highlights low-cost open-source alternatives enabling under-resourced cities; identifies critical research gaps in scalability and species transferability.
why_nature: Policy-urgent topic with SDG relevance at planetary scale; synthesizes fragmented literature into an actionable decision framework for researchers and policymakers; equity dimension linking monitoring data gaps to environmental justice; forward-looking technology assessment relevant to Nature Sustainability's urban sustainability mission.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatially-aware ML classifier recommending optimal sensor combinations given city tree inventory density and sensor budget, connecting to my explainable GeoAI methods
Coupling ground-level and green-roof remote sensing into a 3-D urban green health surface for spatiotemporal kernel density estimation
LiDAR/IRT/HMI-derived tree health attributes as mechanistic replacements for coarse NDVI proxies in my urban heat exposure and health geography spatial regression models
IoT tree sensor co-location with air quality sensors enabling real-time effective greenspace quality inputs for my R2SFCA health-accessibility models
Spatial bias correction pipeline for citizen-science tree health data, directly paralleling my multi-source data fusion methods for sampling-biased urban datasets

related.title: Remote sensing for healthy vegetation on green roofs
related.relation: satellite multispectral/NDVI monitoring of urban green infrastructure — complements street-level sensor review with rooftop remote sensing

related.title: A strong but uneven increase in urban tree cover in China over the recent decade
related.relation: large-scale urban tree cover monitoring across thousands of trees in diverse Chinese cities — test bed for transferable ML method-selection models

related.title: The role of urban trees in reducing land surface temperatures in European cities
related.relation: empirical benchmark for cooling attribution by species and health status — validates UHI mitigation use case in the review

related.title: Green infrastructure can limit but not solve air pollution injustice
related.relation: air quality co-benefits of urban greenery distributed unequally — motivates IoT co-monitoring of tree health and air quality

related.title: Improved human greenspace exposure equality during 21st century urbanization
related.relation: greenspace data gaps track socioeconomic gradients — underscores citizen-science spatial sampling bias problem identified in the review

related.title: Strategically growing the urban forest will improve our world
related.relation: SDG-linked urban forest ecosystem services and growth priorities — provides policy context for the review's SDG use cases

figures.caption: Space-time resolution of sensing method classes (manual, embedded sensors, imaging-based) mapped to SDG requirements — the central decision framework of the review

Three imaging-based methods: thermal image (FLIR Lepton 3.5 at 8-14 micrometres), multispectral image (MAPIR Survey 3W at 660/550/850 nm), and LiDAR point cloud (Apple iPhone 12 Pro + Polycam 3D Scanner)
